speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me that you were liberated and you got back to Buczacz.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother came, and we stayed with Slavka. And then she found an apartment for, for us. And then my mother became the person who saw to our food, and I became the child again. And then I met some people. When I was still in the fields, I met a group of Jewish Partisans who actually had guns. It's the same group that my brother organized. There were other Partisans in the forest, and they were liberated. So when we were liberated, there were several hundred people-- not necessarily from my hometown, but from this whole area. Because about 40,000 people died in my home town. About maybe 300 or 400 people could have survived. I don't know how many, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the ones who survived. And many of them were young people from-- that were in the forest, had hiding places in the forest, who were Partisans, who were-- who were taking their revenge during the occupation. Later, these boys could fight back because there was not a question of retaliation anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to know, Kathy, something very important. When people ask, why didn't the Jews fight back? And there was this terrible tragic thing. If you killed one German, they took hundreds and hundreds of people from the ghetto-- men, women, and children-- and they massacred them. Did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon? In our hometown, I didn't see it, but later on, I heard about instances where the Germans were killed. Even a village, I heard, in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, before-- you can tell me that later. Going back to your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came back. And these people who were liberated-- we were walkin' around like shadows, just seeing each other. We hardly talked to each other. We just like mimicked, nobody was left, nobody was left, nobody was left. And then one day, I was standing on the corner near the, the drugstore. And I saw a young girl wearing a fur jacket my friend-- my Jewish friend-- had gotten for her birthday from her father. And it just about broke my heart. And I used to go and, and visit sometimes the police, the prison. And I was happy to see, you know, people were not, you know, really in prison anymore. And my mother-- I saw Milek one time. That's when I found that he survived. But he stayed a very short time. He went back to Stanislaw where he came from to look for his family. And we were liberated a short time-- I think April-- about two months, we were liberated. And we thought, well, this is what it's going to be like, when one night we heard cannon shots and shooting. And we went outside on the street. And we saw people running. What happened is the Germans were coming back. We-- I found out later on that the Russians were in such a hurry to go west, they, they surrounded three German divisions in the city of Tarnopol. The, the, the Germans broke out, and they came back. The, the Russians didn't even tell us that the Germans were coming back-- nothing. We found out that particular night. And so my mother and I were running to go with the Russians to go away from the Germans. And my mother was hit by a piece of shrapnel in her thigh, was very badly wounded. So we couldn't run any more. We finally got back to our home. And then the second nightmare started all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother was very ill because she was-- and they didn't have medicine for her. I did see an ambulance. And I would have gone in and maybe stolen some medicine for her. But then I thought, if I am caught, what is going to happen to my mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the person in charge of this building where people lived was a Polish woman. And a few days later, after the Germans came in, she went to the police. And one day, two SS men came in, into our apartment. And they motioned us to go out in, in the yard. And they-- we-- I, I helped my mother-- was holding up my mother. And one of them pulled out the gun and shot at me. And my mother threw herself in front of me. And she, she was killed. And then he shot again. And he didn't have a bullet. There was no bullet in his, in his gun. I didn't-- I don't know why the second one didn't kill me, but they took me to the prison. And then I found other people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, before you tell me that. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last words to me, 'You must live, Alicia.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What? At that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had fury in my heart. But I, I think I was also in in shock. You can't help it to see something like that in front of your eyes. I just stood there. You know, I didn't run. I didn't do anything, just holding up my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was shot through the heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. When he hit me and they motioned me to go out, to go, to follow, in the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you say anything? Did you touch her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I kissed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what happened? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's almost indescribable. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It's almost indescribable. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was taken to prison. And then other people were there because they were betrayed. They didn't have a chance to hide. And the good neighbors called the police. And they betrayed them. And among them was a young girl, was my brother Zachary's friend. And I knew this was the prison-- when I was there already before when they took me Czortkow-- that the place they will probably take us is to the [? Fedor-- ?] to this forest. And that's where they will kill us. Their favorite burial place for us killing was near the football place, where they played soccer. That area, the route to the forest, there was a sloping hill. And it was planted with pine trees. I knew that area because during the ghetto times, I used to go through there, go through the river into the forest, and pick up the branches. The Poles used to steal-- cut down the trees, steal the wood. And they left the branches. And we kids used to flow them through. And once the Polish kids ambushed us and took the branches away from us. And I was able to hide myself in a, in a tree that-- a hollow tree that was hollowed out from the side of the river. It was growing between the river and and in the foot of the hill. From the river, the, the water hollowed it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so I told Ava, I told this girl, I said, look, they're going-- if they're going to kill us-- we-- I, I know if they're killing us in that area, I know how to escape. And I know where to hide. Now, just make sure. Follow me. We will hold hands. And we will-- and follow me. But I have to rest. I have to close my eyes. Because I knew the only way I can survive and face anything after having seen my mother cruelly murdered in front of my eyes-- that if I close my eyes, and I, and I sleep, maybe I will be able to wake up and, and function. When I woke up and I told Ava about this, she had-- during that time when I was sleeping-- spaced herself out of life. I couldn't reach her at all. And so we would walk. I kept telling her, now remember what is going to happen. We are going to run when we get there. And this is what you are going to do. And you will follow me. If we are separated, you follow me. Run after me. I know where to hide. And I told her about this tree and everything. But then we came to the grave. She was pulling me towards the grave. I was pulling her away. She was pulling me-- she was completely-- her mind had blanked out, totally. She'd separated herself from the pain and from the process of dying, of everything. And this happened to quite a few people I found out later on. Now she was about 18 at the time, and I was 13 at the-- I was already 14. I had my birthday. So there was a difference of, of us viewing things. And so I pulled her. And then finally, we got separated. And I thought, this is-- she is never going to follow me. She was just going with everybody. And I sprinted. And, and I, and I did get into the tree, and hid there then other people were running too. People were escaping. And then I heard dogs. And I heard shouting. But I hid in that hollow tree through the day. And I was very, very cold. And then I swam over. And I continued going there. And that was when the second occupation started. And the front was not far from there. And I was hiding with all of these refugees. And then I came to a clearing. People had to escape from the front area. And they became refugees. They were Ukrainians and Poles. And we were all sitting in that clearing. There was an older man there. He had some flour. And he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Near what city was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buczacz. And he was making these little pancakes, and he even gave me one. I don't know if he knew I was a Jewish girl or not, but there were children there. So  I was sitting with them together. The following morning, I took a little walk and wanted to see about the forest, wanted to find out maybe I will have to hide in the forest to escape. But as I was coming near the forest, I saw a silhouette. I saw somebody standing there. To, to go to the, the story, to the eye of the story, I found it was a Jewish boy who was hiding in this forest. And I was talking to him. And as I was talking to him, I turned around. And I saw a mounted, mounted soldier on the horse who came into this area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the old man came up to him and was pointing in my direction. He was saying something to him. I didn't hear what he was saying. And-- but I immediately got suspicious. Because why should he send this, this mounted soldier in my direction? So I thought maybe he was-- this is exactly what it is. He says, he was, he was a soldier, an officer, a cavalry officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came up where the area was. And I told the boy to run into the forest. I would have run, too. But he saw me there. And if I ran into the forest, and he was an SS man, he would have gone in the forest and most likely found both of us. So I had to make a decision. One of us has to escape, and the boy was the, the, the likely candidate because--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So he came up, and he asked me to sit on the ground. And he was mounted on the horse. And I-- as I sat down on the ground, I kind of pulled my leg out close to his horse. Because I was, I, I was a very good rider. And I said to myself, well, if he is going to shoot me, I'm going to try to, to hit his horse. And maybe I will dismount him or something. Then he looked down on me. We looked at each other. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH]. You innocent girl. And he rode away. He was just a lost soldier himself. He just didn't want to shoot me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I was very angry and very hurt because here was this man-- I mean, there was this soldier-- if it was at least an SS man, I-- but he was this soldier. He wasn't looking for Jews. He was lost. He was lost. He came into that clearing. And so I had a few things. I went, and I picked them up. And I just walked away. And I walked around in that forest and around. Then the shock of my mother's death set in and being betrayed like that, and worrying, looking for this boy, and not finding him because I didn't know what happened to him, and I was just going in circles and circles. And then one night, I had a dream. And my mother was talking to me. She said, get up, Alicia. Get going, get going. And finally, I got out of that forest. And I followed-- instead of going toward Buczacz, I was away-- was going away from Buczacz. And I thought, I'm going to go to Western Poland. I will just go, and go, and go west. And maybe wherever I will end up, maybe I will be able to pretend I was a refugee, or a Polish girl, or Ukrainian girl, any one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was working in the fields again. And then how I met the-- and then I came to the city of Przemysl on the outskirts, where the, the train, the train station. And I saw a train. And I saw boys and girls. And the girls were dressed up in festive Ukrainian clothes. And there was a pump there. And I wanted to drink some water. And there was a girl drinking there. And I asked in Ukrainian where, where she was going. And they said that they are going to work on, on German farms. They were taken to Germany. And she was so friendly. And I was so lonely. And when she was going to the train, I followed her. And when they pulled her into the train, I put up my hands. They pulled me in. I was sitting there with these boys and girls going to these farms. They didn't really know who I was, because I spoke a perfect Ukrainian. And the train just started moving. They closed the doors, started moving. Then suddenly, we heard explosions. You see this train had several cars with boys and girls going to, to the-- to Germany to work. But some of the passenger cars were filled with SS Gestapo. The Partisans, the Kovpak partisans found out. And they, and they blew up those cars. And there was a massacre there. And then, somebody opened the door. And we ran out into the ditch. And while I was running, I got a piece of shrapnel in two places-- in one in my leg in here in-- and in my tush. I still have the piece there inside me. And we were in that ditch. And then they gathered up all the boys and girls and put us in, in, in-- downstairs in the basement in basement room in the prison. And they were going to check the documents. Because they believed that it was somebody from this train that told the Partisans that there are Germans in the car-- all kind of story. And then I thought I was in terrible trouble. Because, you see, I didn't have any documents to identify who I was. And who doesn't have documents and the way I was dressed, I wasn't really looking like a girl going to Germany. And so I told, told them a story that I ran away from my cruel stepmother, and that I left my little brother, and that I should get out of here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a little window, which was very difficult to open. And they opened that window, which [? fall-- ?] the window opened inside. Can you imagine what kind of terrible thing it is? Anyway, they heaved me up. And I crawled out of this window. And I was already wounded, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was crawling, I looked in in another window. And I saw there people wearing khaki with bandages all over them. And I, I realized immediately that these must be some partisans, some people who have-- you know, we knew already the story-- that were caught wounded. And I knocked on that window, and knocked on that window, and knocked on that window. And I was frightened because any time they could catch me. And finally, somebody came to that window-- had the bandaged head-- and I motioned them that I will pull them out. Well, they pried that window, and I pulled eight out of them, eight partisans out of this window. They told--

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian-- Russian Partisans. And then I asked them to go with them because I knew already from the first time that there were Partisans in this area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know their names. And so I asked them to take me along. They say they can't take a wounded girl along. And they disappeared into the forest. And then I started walking. I went through this forest. And then I tried to find some place where I could be a shepherdess or work in the fields. And then finally, I found a home where they took me in as a shepherdess. I worked day and night, worked in the fields, worked [? in a ?] [? shepherd. ?] And that was not far from the front, from the Russian-German front. And I was in that village. And the girl who-- the daughter of the house wasn't very happy for me to be there. And she-- I was nearly starving if it wasn't for her two little twin brothers. They were very kind to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the fall of 1944. It was September, 1944, October, 1944. If you could hear shooting, it was not far from the Hungarian border. It was in [NON-ENGLISH] in a small village there. And then the Germans came and caught-- wanted the girls, the village girls, to come and peel potatoes for their field kitchen. And the farmer was terribly afraid of his beautiful daughter to go and work in this field kitchen to peel potatoes. And they kind of volunteered me. And I said, well, he is giving me a place to be. And I will go instead of the girl. Because after all, I got fine-- skinny again. I had gained some weight. And I had lost some weight, and my clothes wasn't exactly what you would call--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went with German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- no. Yeah. So I went to that kitchen with other girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: They never suspected you of being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't suspect me. They didn't care. They didn't pay any attention. They were combat soldiers. And I was peeling potatoes, because I had another reason. If I could get the peels and I could make a little fire and I could, you know, bake the peels, and I have some extra food. And we were peeling there. And then I-- and the farmers were so-- the, the, the father and mother of this girl were so afraid that the Germans will do something to this girl. And so they were very happy if I volunteered in her place. And I wanted these potato peels, so I had my alternate motive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went. And I was sitting there with the other girls and peeling the potatoes. And then when-- after they cooked the soup, they had this big cauldron. And then they said, well, now you have to wash it. And none of the girls wanted to go in in there, and wash-- and inside to wash it. And they volunteered me. They said, you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in. And I sat in there. And on the brim of this thing were wonderful vegetables. They were all caught there, you know? And there I was sitting [LAUGHS] instead of cleaning this thing. I was eating my fill. The following day, nobody had to volunteer. I was very happy to get into this cauldron and get myself all these wonderful vegetables. The cauldron was empty, but the vegetables were stuck around the brim. And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was working there for about three days. Then, one afternoon, after-- when I was going to wash this thing out, there were some soldiers who picked up their soups and were sitting and eating. And they picked them up in these jars. I heard them speaking. And one of them said in German, I [? under. ?] They said that finally, they found all of these partisans. And they're going to be surrounded. And they finally get rid of these damn partisans. And, and they were going on, and on, on the partisans. And they have to, they have to hurry up, because they are going to participate in, in murdering all of these Russian Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I heard this sort of thing-- you see they weren't just ordinary Russian partisans. Among them must have been my eight guys that I pulled out of there. They were already my people, people I was responsible for. I felt that they were my people. And what I did-- the stupidest thing ever I did in my life, truly the stupidest thing-- I, I left the whole thing. I didn't clean the cauldron. I ran like the devil was chasing me, caught the first horse in the field, mounted that horse, went into the forest. And I thought like this, I will never find the partisans. But they will find me. And when I dismounted the horse, the minute I dismounted, I didn't even have a chance to look for anybody. I felt a gun in my back. And thank God something made me speak Russian instead of German or any other language. And I said, I say, don't shoot. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] Don't shoot. I am a Jewish girl. You are surrounded. Run! And he-- they-- he disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: By- by the time I got-- it started to rain. By the time I got my horse and wanted to leave the forest, the forest was filled with Ukrainian police and Germans. And they came up to me. And the Ukrainian says what was I doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to cry. And I say, my father beats me. The horse ran away. I didn't milk my cows. And then I was hitting the horse. I say, that stupid horse is going to get me into trouble. And I was crying. I was petrified. And I was blaming it all on the horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say, I don't know how I can get the cows now. They're all in the field. And it's raining. And the German say, [NON-ENGLISH]. What is she saying? And finally, the Ukrainian realized, that's a stupid village girl. And they let me go. [SIGHS] What can I say? I took the horse into the barn. I washed him down and cleaned him off. And this is the first time I saw a horse cry. You know horses cry? I hugged him, and I said, I'm sorry if I hit you. I just had to blame somebody, because I was so frightened. But the, the partisans escaped. It was very shortly, about maybe a month later-- it was in the month of November when the village was liberated by the Russians. And I was with the cows when one of the boys came and said, they're looking for you. I say, who? He says, the Russians. And he was so petrified. He says, maybe you should hide. I say, no. What happened-- oh, by the way, during that period of time I was in the village, one day I was out with the cows. I saw a head going up and down the rows of the potatoes-- a straw head going down and pulling the potatoes. And I said to myself, who could be stealing potatoes? Oh, Jews or partisans. So after this head disappeared, I took all the potato stalks. And I put them right back into the earth. Because I was afraid, you know, the farmer sees somebody was stealing his potatoes, they would go to the Germans. And before you know it, it's trouble. So I put them all back. Well, anyway, [LAUGHS] later on I found out it was one of the partisans stealing the potatoes. So-- So now talking about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so they, they asked me to go. And I didn't make a fuss, because after all, the Russians are not going to kill me. They brought me to this school, the same school where, where they had this kitchen. And I walked in there. And it was-- they were sitting there. And, and this, this boy who brought-- this soldier who brought me there didn't have epaulets. I didn't know if he was an officer. He was just wearing a regular Russian uniform. And they said, is this the girl, one of the high-ranking officer. And he said, yes. And then the joy started. They slapped me on the back. And, of course, everything hurt me. And they, they were drinking a little bit. They were very jolly. And they were, [NON-ENGLISH], very brave girl. And they tell the whole story. I mean, among-- this boy who brought me was one of the guys I pulled out from that prison. And he was the one who was pulling these potatoes. They knew I was there in this village.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they know that when they described the girl who came to tell them that they are surrounded, they described me. And these boys recognized me. This must of [NON-ENGLISH] that it was a Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did they do? They gave me a medal. They made me a hero of the Soviet Union. And then later on-- now this boy who brought me, he was a Jewish boy. His name was Kolia. Kolia Yarov. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. His father-- he brought me to his father later on. His father was a physician. You see, the first contingency of the Russian partisans, the Kovpak partisans [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. Twice a year of the Soviet Union, the Kovpak, whose partisans are named after him, were totally destroyed because they didn't just fight in the forest, they went and sabotage behind the German lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know-- did you know that at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I found that--

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I found out later. The second Kovpak, the second contingency [INAUDIBLE], were students from universities, men. About 60% of them were Jewish students who volunteered for this suicidal mission. Kolav was a Jew. His father was a Jewy Arab. Quite a few of them were Jews. I didn't know that. I found out later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to your own life.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so they gave me these, these papers. And, well, I asked them to find me a ride that I was going back Buczacz. And so I came back to Buczacz. And then there were very few survivors there. Most of the people who survived the first time were betrayed the second time-- did not survive the second time. Out of about 6,000 children from my hometown, only five children that I know of survived-- four in Israel, and I'm here. There were other children, perhaps, who came in from other parts of the ghetto. But I didn't know them, only the children that I knew personally before the war. One of the girls lived in Jerusalem, and I was friends with her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- you already knew of it-- none of your family members.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So the first thing I did, I arranged with a man there-- a Jewish man-- to find my mother and bring her to Kava Israel. Kathy, I went to the house. I found the woman who betrayed me. My mother was buried there in, in, in, in, in a shallow grave. We put her in a wheelbarrow. We brought her to Kava Israel. And I was facing my mother's murderer. It was the worst time in my life. I was angry. I was furious. Yet, I couldn't really do anything to her. You see, she reached a, a, kind of, level of the lowest form of life. I didn't want to kill her. I didn't want to do anything to her, because to kill her, I would have to become a murderer, this lowest form of life. And I didn't want to be a murderer to this lowest form of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had given you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- she told, she told me where, where my mother was. And she was petrified. She was sure that I was going to kill her, or bring her to the police, or tell the police of the crime she committed, that she sent the Germans on us. And-- but then, from then, that day on, I started spending most of my time on the cemetery. There was my mother buried there with my brother Zachary. There was Rachel, my friend Rachel. And there was a grave I didn't know, but it was so close. So I used to put leaves or flowers on there. And that grave turned out to be Milek's. After the war, Milek came back to Buczacz. And he and his friend Benjamin went to the library to take up books on biology. Milek still wanted to become a physician. And on the way back, they stepped on the bridge. There was a live mine. They stepped on that mine. The mine exploded. Milek was killed instantly, and Benjamin last-- lost half of his leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I found out about Milek and Benjamin. Bella asked me-- Bella survived-- and she asked me to go and talk to Benjamin because he lost half of its leg. And could I play chess with him? He was very depressed. And he was-- had on-- he had a little library with some books. And he had the biology book open there. And I was wondering why he would study biology. Does he want to be a doctor? And he told me about his friend Milek. And this is how I connected it. And you found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found out about-- found out about Milek. And I was devastated. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in love with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. I loved him since the age of five. He gave me one kiss on the cheek and gave me this ring. But I loved Milek all my life. And I knew that my destiny will be-- my future is going to be with Milek. This is-- we are both going to be physicians, and we are going to serve humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going into the next tape now. Thank you.
